3-1.v: Variance analysis is the process of comparing ACTUAL results with BUDGETED (standard) amounts for the same period. It identifies differences so management can investigate causes and take corrective action.

v: Identify areas where performance differs from expectations
Support management by exception — focus on significant variances
Help improve efficiency, cost control, and decision-making

3-1.v: A management system where only SIGNIFICANT deviations from the budget are reported. Benefits: saves time, identifies unfavorable and favorable trends. Limitations: minor but consistent trends may be missed; too many variances can cause confusion.
The predetermined cost of producing one unit under EXPECTED conditions. Components: Direct Materials (Qty × Price), Direct Labor (Hours × Rate), Manufacturing Overhead (Predetermined rate).
Levels of Activity for Standard Costs

r: Ideal minus normal downtime & learning curve
Average expected output over several years
Planned utilization for next budget period
Scientifically measures each task and resource requirement
Low cost, realistic — based on past actuals
May not perfectly fit company's processes
Market-driven — starts with price and works backward
Requires process innovation to hit cost target
Driven by competitor pricing or customer willingness to pay
External; may not reflect internal capabilities
Least detail — compare actual to master budget
Adjusts for actual activity; separates price/efficiency from volume
Most detailed — price, efficiency, mix, yield for each input

v: Revenue/Income: Positive = Favorable (F); Negative = Unfavorable (U)
Costs/Expenses: Positive = Unfavorable (U); Negative = Favorable (F)

3-1.v: Flexible Budget Variance = Actual Results − Flexible Budget
Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget − Static Budget

Exam Tips: Standard costs = predetermined costs under NORMAL conditions. Practical standard = most common. Management by exception = focus on significant variances. Level 1 = static budget. Level 2 = flexible budget + volume. Level 3 = input (price + efficiency + mix + yield). Fixed costs stay same in flexible budgets within relevant range.

3-2.v: Manufacturing input variances are differences between ACTUAL costs and STANDARD costs allowed for the actual level of output. They appear on a PRODUCTION variance report (based on units produced, not sold).
Total DM Variance = (AP × AQ) − (SP × SQ) | AP = Actual Price, AQ = Actual Qty Used, SP = Standard Price, SQ = Standard Qty Allowed for actual output
(AP − SP) × AQ | Positive = Unfavorable (paid more) | Negative = Favorable (paid less)
(AQ − SQ) × SP | Positive = Unfavorable (used more) | Negative = Favorable (used less)
(AP − SP) × Quantity PURCHASED (not used) | Benefits: identifies purchasing problems immediately
Price Variance + Quantity Variance = Total DM Variance

Exam Tips: DM Price Variance uses AQ PURCHASED. DM Quantity Variance uses AQ USED vs SQ ALLOWED for actual output. SQ Allowed = Standard qty per unit × Actual units produced. Both variances use STANDARD PRICE as the common denominator for the quantity variance. Investigate ALL significant variances — both favorable and unfavorable.

3-3.v: Total DL Variance = (AR × AH) − (SR × SH) | AR = Actual Rate, AH = Actual Hours, SR = Standard Rate, SH = Standard Hours Allowed for actual output
(AR − SR) × AH | Positive = Unfavorable (paid more) | Negative = Favorable (paid less)

r: Using higher-skilled workers at premium rates

3-3.v: (AH − SH) × SR | Positive = Unfavorable (more hours used) | Negative = Favorable (fewer hours used)
Total DL Variance = Rate Variance + Efficiency Variance

Exam Tips: DL Rate Variance = actual rate vs standard × actual hours worked. DL Efficiency Variance = actual hours vs standard hours allowed × STANDARD rate. Standard hours allowed = standard hours per unit × actual units produced. Buying cheaper DM may increase DL inefficiency (rework). Variances often interrelated.

3-4.v: When MORE THAN ONE type of direct material or more than one WAGE CLASS of labor is used, the Quantity Variance is further broken into MIX and YIELD variances.

v: Single input: Total → Price + Quantity
Multiple inputs: Total → Price + (Mix + Yield)
Mix Variance = change in proportion of inputs used
Yield Variance = change in total inputs used vs standard

3-4.v: Mix Variance = (WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × Total AQ | WASP_AM = Weighted Avg Std Price of Actual Mix | WASP_SM = Weighted Avg Std Price of Standard Mix
Interpretation: Positive = Unfavorable (using more of HIGH-cost inputs). Negative = Favorable (using more of LOW-cost inputs).

v: WASP for Actual Mix (WASP_AM): Multiply actual qty of each input × its standard price, add all, divide by total actual qty
WASP for Standard Mix (WASP_SM): Multiply standard qty allowed of each input × its standard price, add all, divide by total standard qty allowed

3-4.v: Standard mix for 1,000 units of output: Material A = 600 kg at $4/kg standard; Material B = 400 kg at $10/kg standard. Total standard qty = 1,000 kg. Actual usage: Material A = 500 kg; Material B = 550 kg. Total actual qty = 1,050 kg.
(WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × Total AQ = ($7.143 − $6.40) × 1,050 = $0.743 × 1,050 = $780 UNFAVORABLE (used more high-cost Material B than standard)
(Total AQ − Total SQ) × WASP_SM = (1,050 − 1,000) × $6.40 = 50 × $6.40 = $320 UNFAVORABLE (used 50 kg more total than standard)
Mix $780 U + Yield $320 U = $1,100 Unfavorable — verify: (AP×AQ)−(SP×SQ) method gives same result
Yield Variance = (Total AQ − Total SQ) × WASP_SM | Positive = Unfavorable (used more total inputs) | Negative = Favorable (used fewer total inputs)
Total Quantity Variance = Mix Variance + Yield Variance

r: Cost of each input vs standard

3-4.v: Exam Tips: Mix and Yield only calculated when MORE THAN ONE input type is used. WASP_AM = weighted average std price using actual proportions. WASP_SM = weighted average std price using standard proportions. Favorable mix = using more LOW-cost inputs. Favorable yield = using LESS total input than standard.

3-5.v: Overhead Cost Overview
Variable Overhead Variances
Total VOH Variance = Actual VOH Incurred − Applied VOH | Applied VOH = Standard VOH Rate × Standard Qty of allocation base allowed for actual output
(AP − SP) × AQ | AP = Actual VOH rate, SP = Standard VOH rate, AQ = Actual allocation base used
(AQ − SQ) × SP | AQ = Actual allocation base used, SQ = Standard base allowed for actual output, SP = Standard VOH rate
VOH Efficiency Variance: measures efficiency in using the ALLOCATION BASE (e.g., DL hours). If labor hours are the base, same causes as DL Efficiency Variance.
VOH Spending Variance + VOH Efficiency Variance = Total VOH Variance
Fixed Overhead Variances
Total FOH Variance = Actual FOH Incurred − Applied FOH | Applied FOH = Standard FOH rate × Standard usage for actual output
Actual FOH − Budgeted FOH | Positive = Unfavorable (spent more than budgeted)
Budgeted FOH − Applied FOH | Positive = Unfavorable (produced less than planned — under-absorbed FOH)
FOH Volume Variance: NO link to actual costs — purely measures CAPACITY UTILIZATION. It shows how well fixed costs are absorbed based on actual vs planned output.
FOH Spending Variance + FOH Production Volume Variance = Total FOH Variance
Total Overhead Flexible Budget Variance
= Actual Total OH − Total Flexible Budget OH for Actual Output | Includes: VOH Spending + VOH Efficiency + FOH Spending | EXCLUDES: FOH Production Volume Variance
2-Variance, 3-Variance, and 4-Variance Methods Compared
The IMA tests all three overhead analysis methods. They differ only in how much detail is reported — all three arrive at the same total overhead variance.

r: (1) Budget (Controllable) Variance = VOH Spending + VOH Efficiency + FOH Spending  |  (2) Production Volume Variance = FOH Volume
Least detail — combines all controllable variances into one
(1) Spending Variance = VOH Spending + FOH Spending  |  (2) Efficiency Variance = VOH Efficiency  |  (3) Production Volume Variance = FOH Volume
(1) VOH Spending  (2) VOH Efficiency  (3) FOH Spending  (4) FOH Production Volume
Most detail — each variance reported separately

3-5.v: Actual Total OH − Flexible Budget OH for Actual Output | = VOH Spending + VOH Efficiency + FOH Spending
Flexible Budget FOH − Applied FOH | Same as the 4-variance FOH Volume Variance

Exam Tips: VOH Spending = actual vs standard RATE per base unit. VOH Efficiency = allocation base efficiency. FOH Spending = actual vs BUDGETED fixed cost. FOH Volume = capacity utilization (no connection to actual spending). FOH Volume Unfavorable = produced LESS than planned. The Flexible Budget OH Variance EXCLUDES FOH Volume Variance. Know all three methods (2-, 3-, 4-variance) — the IMA tests all of them.

3-6.v: Sales variances measure differences between ACTUAL and BUDGETED results for revenues, variable costs, and contribution margin — based on UNITS SOLD (not produced). Unlike manufacturing variances which are based on production.

r: Impact of selling different units than planned
Effect of charging different price than budget

3-6.v: Flexible Budget Variance + Sales Volume Variance = Static Budget Variance
(AP − SP) × AQ | AP = Actual price, SP = Standard/budgeted price, AQ = Actual qty sold | Positive = FAVORABLE (sold at higher price)
(AQ − SQ) × SP | AQ = Actual qty sold, SQ = Budgeted qty, SP = Budgeted selling price | Positive = FAVORABLE (sold more units)
Apply same formulas but SP replaced with budgeted variable cost per unit (for costs) or budgeted CM per unit (for CM). For costs: negative variance = Favorable (lower actual cost).
Multi-Product: Sales Quantity and Mix Variances
(AQ total − SQ total) × WASP_SM | WASP_SM = Weighted Avg Std Price for Standard Mix
(WASP_AM − WASP_SM) × AQ total | WASP_AM = Weighted Avg Std Price for Actual Mix
Sales Quantity Variance + Sales Mix Variance = Total Sales Volume Variance

Exam Tips: Sales variances based on UNITS SOLD (not produced). Selling Price Variance: positive = Favorable (opposite to cost variances). Sales Volume Variance: use BUDGETED CM per unit for contribution margin analysis. Multi-product: Volume splits into Quantity + Mix variances.

3-7.v: Market variances break down the SALES QUANTITY VARIANCE (for multi-product companies) or SALES VOLUME VARIANCE (for single-product companies) into two components based on EXTERNAL market data:

v: Market Size Variance (MSV): Change due to OVERALL MARKET DEMAND SIZE
Market Share Variance (MSHV): Change due to company's PROPORTION of the market

3-7.v: Market Size Variance + Market Share Variance = Sales Quantity Variance (multi-product) OR Sales Volume Variance (single product)
(Actual Market Size − Expected Market Size) × Expected Market Share % × WASCMP_SM | WASCMP_SM = Weighted Avg Standard CM per unit for Standard Mix
Interpretation: Positive (F) = market GREW beyond expectations — more opportunity. Negative (U) = market CONTRACTED.
(Actual Market Share − Expected Market Share) × Actual Market Size × WASCMP_SM
Interpretation: Positive (F) = gained MORE market share than planned. Negative (U) = LOST market share to competitors.

v: Separate EXTERNAL factors (market size) from INTERNAL factors (market share)
A company can beat its sales budget only because the market grew (MSV favorable) but lose share (MSHV unfavorable) — this is a warning sign
Conversely, a company may miss budget because the market shrank, yet gained share — a positive internal performance signal

3-7.v: Exam Tips: MSV = external factor (industry demand change). MSHV = internal factor (competitive position). MSV favorable = industry grew. MSHV favorable = company captured more of the market. Together they explain the Sales Quantity Variance. Use CONTRIBUTION MARGIN per unit (not selling price) for multi-product analysis.

3-8.v: A system that measures FINANCIAL RESULTS of each responsibility center separately, allowing combination of all centers' results to evaluate total company performance. Links PLANNING (budgets) with CONTROL (actual vs budget).

v: Purpose: Evaluate performance of subunits and managers
Provides motivation through clear accountability
Supports strategic decision-making
Key principle: Evaluate managers ONLY on what they CAN CONTROL

3-8.v: KEY PRINCIPLE: Evaluate managers only on what THEY CAN CONTROL. A unit may underperform due to factors outside the manager's control. Evaluate: (1) Manager on controllable items, (2) Unit on ALL traceable items.

r: Manager can directly influence during the period

3-8.v: Common Costs vs. Shared Services Costs

v: Common Costs: Cannot be traced to a specific department on a cause-effect basis. Remain even if department is eliminated. Example: CEO salary.
Shared Services: Support functions used by multiple units, allocated based on usage. Example: IT, payroll, maintenance.

r: Each unit treated as independent; allocate based on proportion of usage
Rank units by size; primary bears most cost; remainder allocated to others

3-8.v: Exam Tips: 4 types of responsibility centers: Cost, Revenue, Profit, Investment. Investment center = ROI or RI. Manager evaluation = CONTROLLABLE costs only. Unit evaluation = ALL traceable costs. Common costs remain if unit eliminated. Shared services = allocated by usage. Stand-alone method = proportional allocation.

3-9.v: Classifies costs by BEHAVIOR (variable vs. fixed) rather than by function. Key difference from traditional (absorption) income statement: separates fixed costs and focuses on CONTRIBUTION MARGIN.
MCM = Net Revenues − Variable Manufacturing Costs | Variable Mfg Costs = DM + DL + Variable MOH
Shows funds available after covering variable manufacturing costs. Increases in MCM directly improve profit once fixed costs are covered.
CM = MCM − Variable Non-Manufacturing Costs | Variable Non-Mfg = Variable selling, marketing, admin
Core profitability indicator — funds available to cover ALL fixed costs after ALL variable costs.
Controllable Margin = CM − Controllable Fixed Costs | Controllable Fixed = Costs manager can influence in < 1 year (e.g., supervisors' salaries, segment-specific advertising)
Measures SHORT-TERM manager performance. Excludes costs the manager cannot influence.

Segment Margin = Controllable Margin − Non-Controllable Traceable Fixed Costs | Non-Controllable Traceable = Traceable to segment but NOT under manager's short-term control (facility depreciation, property taxes)
Measures LONG-TERM segment performance. Used for decisions about keeping, expanding, or eliminating the segment. NON-CONTROLLABLE TRACEABLE costs WOULD BE ELIMINATED if the segment were closed.
Profit Margin Ratio = Net Income / Revenue | Improve by: increase sales, reduce costs, or increase sales faster than costs

Exam Tips: CIS has 4 levels: MCM → CM → Controllable Margin → Segment Margin. MCM = Revenue − Variable Mfg costs. CM = MCM − Variable Non-Mfg. Controllable = CM − controllable fixed. Segment = Controllable − non-controllable traceable fixed. Non-controllable traceable fixed costs are ELIMINATED if segment is closed.

3-10.v: A transfer price is the price charged for goods or services EXCHANGED BETWEEN SUB-UNITS of the same organization. The exchanged item is called an INTERMEDIATE PRODUCT.

v: Selling division records REVENUE
Buying division records COST
At corporate level: internal transaction ELIMINATED — no effect on consolidated net income
But: affects DIVISIONAL profits → impacts performance evaluation of managers

3-10.v: Objectives of a Transfer Pricing System

v: 1. Promote GOAL CONGRUENCE — align divisional and company goals
2. Allow senior management to EVALUATE PERFORMANCE fairly
3. PRESERVE AUTONOMY in decentralized organizations
4. Motivate managers to control costs and use resources efficiently
5. Meet LEGAL/TAX requirements (arm's-length pricing for international)
6. Be PRACTICAL and easy to administer

r: Buyer has no cost advantage vs external
Outlay Cost + CM lost from forgone external sales
Seller at market; buyer at variable cost
Divisional profits don't add to corporate total

v: Tax authorities require ARM'S-LENGTH pricing (e.g., US IRC §482)
Companies cannot artificially shift income to low-tax jurisdictions
Customs: low transfer prices reduce import duties but invite scrutiny

3-10.v: Min Transfer Price = Outlay Cost + Opportunity Cost (CM lost from forgone external sales) | If seller at full capacity: must cover variable cost + lost CM | If seller has excess capacity: price ≥ variable cost

Exam Tips: At FULL CAPACITY: min price = variable cost + lost CM from external sale. At EXCESS CAPACITY: min price = variable cost. Market price = most objective. Dual-rate: good for both divisions but divisional profits don't add up. International: arm's-length required. Transfer price affects divisional profits but NOT consolidated company profit.

3-11.v: Performance measurement systems must ALIGN manager incentives with organizational objectives (goal congruence). Key issues: measures must link to strategy, balance short vs long-term, and motivate company-wide optimization.

v: Risk: Cutting R&D boosts short-term profit but harms long-term competitiveness
Timing: Profit center managers need frequent data; top management needs summaries

3-11.v: ROI = Annual Operating Income / Assets Employed

r: Compare divisions or projects; assess vs Required Rate of Return (RRR/Hurdle Rate)
Often based on Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
Widely understood, allows comparison across units
Managers may REJECT profitable projects if project ROI < current division ROI (even if > RRR)
Encourages short-termism — cut long-term investments to boost short-term ROI
Distorted by accounting policy choices (inventory, depreciation)

3-11.v: RI = Operating Income − (Assets Employed × Required Rate of Return) | RI > 0 → Investment beneficial

r: Encourages acceptance of all projects with ROI > RRR (unlike ROI metric)
Different RRRs can be set for different risk levels
Varying investment charges by asset class
Not scale-neutral — larger units may have higher RI due to size alone
Still affected by accounting policy differences

3-11.v: Accounting Policy Effects on ROI & RI

v: Inventory valuation (FIFO vs LIFO) affects income and assets
Depreciation method affects asset book value and expense
Asset capitalization thresholds
Use of full costing vs variable costing
Disposition of manufacturing variances
Inflation and recession conditions vary by country
Currency exchange rates affect reported results
Tariffs, trade regulations differ
Solution: Adjust performance metrics for local operating conditions

r: Evaluate using LOCAL CURRENCY results — measure performance in the subsidiary's home currency to eliminate FX noise
Adjust for local inflation rate — compare real (inflation-adjusted) growth, not nominal figures
Separate CONTROLLABLE factors (manager decisions) from NON-CONTROLLABLE factors (macro environment, exchange rate movements)
Use CURRENT REPLACEMENT COST of assets rather than historical cost for comparability

3-11.v: EVA is a refinement of Residual Income that uses after-tax figures and market-based values. It measures whether the company creates or destroys value above the true cost of all capital employed.
EVA = NOPAT − (WACC × Invested Capital) | NOPAT = Net Operating Profit After Tax | Invested Capital = total capital at market (or adjusted book) value

r: NOPAT (after-tax) — RI typically uses operating income (pre-tax)
WACC (market-derived) — RI may use a simpler hurdle rate
Invested capital, often adjusted to market value — RI typically uses book value
EVA > 0 = value created; EVA < 0 = value destroyed — same logic as positive RI
Shareholder value analysis and executive compensation plans

3-11.v: Exam Tips: ROI = Operating Income / Assets. RI = Operating Income − (Assets × RRR). ROI problem: managers reject good projects if project ROI < current ROI. RI advantage: accept all projects where RI > 0 (project ROI > RRR). Larger divisions naturally have higher RI — not scale-neutral. Both measures affected by accounting policies. EVA = NOPAT − (WACC × Invested Capital) — more rigorous than RI; uses after-tax profit and market-based capital. Multinational: evaluate in LOCAL CURRENCY to remove FX distortion.

3-12.v: Traditional financial performance evaluation (quarterly profits) drives short-term thinking and neglects long-term strategy. The BALANCED SCORECARD (BSC) addresses this by integrating financial AND non-financial measures.
Developed by KAPLAN & NORTON (1992). A performance management framework that translates strategic plans into a set of linked financial and operational objectives, measures, targets, and initiatives.

r: Operating income, revenue growth, ROI, RI, gross margin %
Market share %, satisfaction scores, repeat customers %, on-time delivery
Improve operations to create customer value
Cycle time, production cost/unit, new products launched, defect rates
Employee training hours, staff turnover, IT uptime, engagement scores

3-12.v: The perspectives are HIERARCHICAL — improvements in lower-level perspectives support higher-level objectives. Financial perspective = OUTCOME; others are LEAD INDICATORS (drivers).

v: Critical Success Factors (CSFs): Key activities essential to achieving strategic goals — must be limited in number and tied to strategy
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Quantitative measures for CSFs. Example: if 'Customer Service Excellence' is a CSF, KPI could be 'Average call wait time < 30 seconds'
Strategy Map: Visual cause-and-effect diagram — Learning → Internal Process → Customer → Financial

r: Difficult to compare across business units (custom scorecards)
Links non-financial drivers to future financial results
If non-financial targets don't lead to financial gains, measures must be re-evaluated

v: Pareto principle: 80% of profits from 20% of customers
Identify high-value customers for retention and growth
Use ABC for product-level profitability
Relevant costs: avoidable = relevant; unavoidable = irrelevant when evaluating dropping a customer/product

3-12.v: Tableau de Bord — French Predecessor to the BSC

The Tableau de Bord (French for 'dashboard') was developed in France in the early 20th century — predating the Balanced Scorecard by decades. The IMA tests students' ability to distinguish it from the BSC.

r: No fixed framework — management selects any financial and non-financial KPIs relevant to their unit
Fixed 4-perspective framework: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth
Not explicitly linked to a strategy map or cause-and-effect hierarchy
Explicitly linked — strategy map shows cause-and-effect from L&G to Financial
Can be cascaded by unit or level
Designed to cascade from corporate to business unit to individual
Highly flexible — each manager designs their own dashboard
More standardized — perspectives and linkages are defined
Like the BSC, uses both financial and non-financial measures
Like TdB, avoids over-reliance on financial-only metrics

3-12.v: Exam Tips: BSC = 4 perspectives: Financial (outcome), Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth (foundation). Financial = lagging indicator. Others = leading indicators. Learning enables Internal Process → enables Customer → drives Financial. CSF = what must go right. KPI = how we measure it. Strategy Map = visual cause-and-effect. Tableau de Bord = French predecessor; flexible, no fixed framework — contrast with BSC's structured 4-perspective approach.

3-13.v: Financial statement analysis evaluates a company's performance, financial health, and value using ratios computed from the financial statements. The CMA exam tests ratio calculation AND interpretation. Ratios must be compared to: prior periods (trends), industry averages, and specific benchmarks.
1. Liquidity Ratios — Can the company pay short-term obligations?

r: Higher = more liquidity. Below 1.0 = current liabilities exceed current assets — concern
Stricter — excludes inventory. Better for businesses with slow inventory
Most conservative — only most liquid assets
Uses actual cash generated — best liquidity measure

3-13.v: 2. Activity / Efficiency Ratios — How well are assets being used?

r: Higher = faster collection. Low = slow collection or bad debts
Average days to collect payment. Lower is better
Higher = faster moving inventory. Low = slow-moving or obsolete stock
Revenue generated per dollar of assets. Higher = more efficient
How quickly the company pays suppliers

3-13.v: 3. Profitability Ratios — How much profit does the company generate?

r: % of sales remaining after COGS. Higher = pricing power or cost efficiency
Bottom-line profitability per dollar of sales
Profit generated per dollar of assets
Return to common shareholders — key investor metric

3-13.v: ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier | = (NI/Sales) × (Sales/Assets) × (Assets/Equity)
DuPont shows the THREE DRIVERS of ROE: profitability (margin), efficiency (turnover), and financial leverage (equity multiplier). A company can improve ROE by improving any of the three factors.
4. Leverage / Solvency Ratios — Long-term financial risk

r: Higher = more leverage/risk. Creditors prefer lower
Higher = more capacity to pay interest. Below 1.5 = concerning
Growth expectations. Higher P/E = higher growth expected
Accounting value per share — compare to market price

3-13.v: Exam Tips: Liquidity — Current, Quick, Cash (progressively stricter). Activity — turnover ratios (higher = better). Convert to days = 365/turnover. Profitability — margins and returns. DuPont = Margin × Turnover × Leverage. Solvency — D/E, interest coverage (higher coverage = safer). Compare ratios to prior periods AND industry averages. Context always matters — a 'good' ratio depends on the industry.
